elements.sent-2411-001: We carry out a detailed quantitative analysis on the geometry of invariant manifolds for smooth dissipative systems in dimension two.
elements.sent-2411-002: We begin by quantifying the regularity of any orbit (finite or infinite) in the phase space with a set of explicit inequalities.
elements.sent-2411-003: Then we relate this directly to the quasi-linearization of the local dynamics on regular neighborhoods of this orbit.
elements.sent-2411-004: The parameters of regularity explicitly determine the sizes of the regular neighborhoods and the smooth norms of the corresponding regular charts.
elements.sent-2411-005: As a corollary, we establish the existence of smooth stable and center manifolds with uniformly bounded geometries for regular orbits independently of any pre-existing invariant measure.
elements.sent-2411-006: This provides us with the technical background for the renormalization theory of H\'enon-like maps developed in \cite{CLPY1 , \cite{CLPY2 and \cite{Y .}}}
elements.sent-2411-007: In 1970's, Pesin established the foundations for the study of non-uniformly hyperbolic dynamical systems through a series of landmark works \cite{Pe1 ,\cite{Pe2 ,\cite{Pe3 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: The classical implementation of this technique reveals statistical information about the system under consideration with respect to a given invariant measure.
elements.sent-2411-009: However, for many applications, it is important to apply the core mechanisms of Pesin theory without having a pre-established invariant measure, and, in particular, without having the asymptotic information provided by Oseledec's theorem.
elements.sent-2411-010: In this paper, we attempt to lay the groundwork for such a theory.
elements.sent-2411-011: A similar type of approach was developed in \cite{CP and \cite{HL .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Let $r\geq 2$ be an integer, and consider a $C^{r+1}$ surface diffeomorphism $F$.
elements.sent-2411-013: We begin by formulating the relevant regularity conditions that control dominating and hyperbolic features of any finite or infinite orbit.
elements.sent-2411-014: Consider a point $p$ in the surface and a tangent direction $E_p$ at $p$.
elements.sent-2411-015: To quantify the regularity of $p$, we introduce the following parameters: $\lambda, \rho, \epsilon \in (0,1)$; $L \geq 1$ and $N, M \in \bbN \cup \{0, \infty\}$.
elements.sent-2411-016: We say that the point $p$ is {\it $N$-times forward $(L, \epsilon, \lambda, \rho)_v$-regular along $E_p$} if, for all $1 \leq n \leq N$, we have \begin{equation}L^{-1}\lambda^{(1+\epsilon)n}\leq \|DF^n|_{E_p}\| \leq L \lambda^{(1-\epsilon)n}\end{equation} and \begin{equation}L^{-1}\rho^{(1+\epsilon)n}\leq \frac{\|DF^n|_{E_p}\|^2}{\Jac_pF^n} \leq L \rho^{(1-\epsilon)n}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-017: Intuitively, the former condition gives exponential contraction along $E_p$, and the latter gives the domination of this contraction (there is exponential repulsion in the projective tangent space of other directions from $E_p$).
elements.sent-2411-018: Similarly, $p$ is {\it $M$-times backward $(L, \epsilon, \lambda, \rho)_v$-regular along $E_p \in \bbP^2_p$} if, for all $1 \leq n \leq M$, we have \begin{equation}L^{-1}\lambda^{(1+\epsilon)n}\leq \|DF^{-n}|_{E_p}\|^{-1} \leq L \lambda^{(1-\epsilon)n}\end{equation} and \begin{equation}L^{-1}\rho^{(1+\epsilon)n}\leq \frac{\Jac_pF^{-n}}{\|DF^{-n}|_{E_p}\|^2} \leq L \rho^{(1-\epsilon)n}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-019: The main technical result of the paper is that if the following conditions hold on the parameters of regularity: \begin{equation}\frac{\rho^{(r+1)-\epsilon(r+3)}}{\lambda^{(1+\epsilon)r}} < 1 \matsp{and} \frac{\lambda^{(1-\epsilon)(r+1)}}{\rho^{r+\epsilon(2-r)}} < 1,\end{equation} then there exist $C^r$ changes-of-co\-or\-di\-na\-tes, called {\it regular charts , defined on slow-exponentially shrinking neighborhoods covering a regular orbit, called {\it regular neighborhoods , that conjugates the local dynamics of $F$ to a nearly linear one.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Moreover, the conjugated maps have a certain skew-product form which is particularly useful for obtaining strong $C^r$-estimates for the dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-021: This construction is referred to as a {\it quasi-linearization (or {\it Q-linearization ) {\it of $F$}, and is given in \secref{sec.chart .}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: As consequences of Q-linearization, we obtain invariant curves with uniformly bounded local geometries that approximate the local stable and center manifolds in case of finite-time regularity, and coincide with them in case of infinite-time regularity.
elements.sent-2411-023: This can be loosely stated as follows (for the precise statements, see Theorems \ref{stable and \ref{center jet ).. \begin{thma Suppose \begin{equation}\frac{\lambda^{1-5\epsilon}}{\rho^{8\epsilon}} < 1 \matsp{and} \frac{\rho^{1-\epsilon}}{\lambda^{2\epsilon}} < 1.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Assume that $p$ is infinite-time forward regular along $E_p$.
elements.sent-2411-025: Then $p$ has a unique $C^r$ strong-stable manifold $W^{ss (p)$, which is tangent to $E_p$.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Additionally, let $W^{ss _{\loc (p) \ni p$ be the connected component of $W^{ss (p)$ intersected with the regular neighborhood at $p$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Then the $C^r$-norm of the arc-length parameterization of $W^{ss _{\loc (p)$ is uniformly bounded in terms of the parameters of regularity of $p$.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Assume that $p$ is infinite-time backward regular along $E_p$.
elements.sent-2411-029: Then $p$ has a $C^r$ center manifold $W^c(p)$ contained in the regular neighborhood at $p$, whose tangent direction $E^c_p$ at $p$ is transverse to $E_p$.
elements.sent-2411-031: Furthermore, the $C^r$-norm of the arc-length parameterization of $W^c(p)$ is uniformly bounded in terms of the parameters of regularity of $p$.
elements.sent-2411-032: In the class of 2D dynamical systems that we consider, each map features a unique orbit of tangencies between strong-stable and center manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-033: We refer to this orbit as the {\it critical orbit .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Note that the critical orbit is highly atypical, and hence is unaccounted for by classical measurable Pesin theory.
elements.sent-2411-035: Nonetheless, it turns out that this single orbit is the primary source of non-linearity for the system, and is chiefly responsible for shaping its overall dynamics (analogously to the critical orbit of a unimodal map in 1D).
elements.sent-2411-036: Quantitative Pesin theory provides an adequate language to analyze and describe how this happens.
elements.sent-2411-037: Although the aforementioned works are specifically about renormalization, the tools developed in this paper are quite general.
elements.sent-2411-038: We expect that they will find applications in other settings as well.
elements.sent-2411-039: In particular, we believe that they will be especially useful in the study of dissipative systems that feature tangencies between stable and center/unstable manifolds, in the spirit of the work of Benedicks and Carleson \cite{BeCa .}
elements.sent-2411-040: Furthermore, it should be commented that we restrict ourselves to the 2D case mainly for simplicity.
elements.sent-2411-041: Our quantitative approach should naturally generalize to arbitrary dimensions.

elements.sent-2411-168: In this section we introduce the notion of regularity, which quantifies the idea of having certain a rate of contraction/expansion in certain directions as well as domination relation between them.
elements.sent-2411-169: We give two definitions, one with respect to the strongly contracting/expanding direction, and another with respect to a transverse direction.
elements.sent-2411-170: Lastly, we show that these definitions are equivalent (up to uniformly increasing the size of the associated errors).
elements.sent-2411-171: This lays down technical background for the quasi-linearization results in the next section.
